explanation_md: `sizeof(char)` is always 1 by the C++ standard. `sizeof(int)` is 4 on virtually all modern 32-bit and 64-bit platforms. The values are guaranteed at compile time and have no runtime cost.

prompt_md: What unit does `sizeof` return values in?

options.d: The number of decimal digits the type can hold

explanation_md: `sizeof` always returns the size in bytes. `sizeof(int)` is typically 4, meaning 4 bytes (32 bits). Option a is a very common mistake — multiplying by 8 gives the bit count.

The C++ standard specifies that `sizeof` returns a value of type `std::size_t`, which is defined in `<cstddef>`. It is an implementation-defined unsigned integer type, typically 8 bytes on 64-bit platforms.

options.a: `sizeof(int)` returns the size in bits, so this is correct.
options.b: `sizeof(int)` returns the size in bytes; to get bits you must multiply by 8.
options.c: `sizeof` does not work on types — only on variables.
options.d: The output label `bits` causes a compile error.

explanation_md: `sizeof` measures bytes, not bits. On a typical platform `sizeof(int)` is 4 (bytes), so the program prints `4 bits` instead of the correct `32 bits`. The fix is `sizeof(int) * 8`.

`sizeof` evaluates the type of its operand without running the expression. `a + b` produces an `int`, so `sizeof(a + b)` is `sizeof(int)`, which is 4 on a typical platform. The addition `10 + 20` never actually executes.
